1: #!/usr/bin/env bash
2: # bench_sprint1.sh
3: # Benchmarks each Sprint 1 PR commit cumulatively.
4: # Resumable: completed steps are skipped on re-run.
5: # Observable: prints [SKIP/BUILD/RUN/DONE/FAIL] per step with timestamps.
6: #
7: # Usage:
8: #   bash test/bench_sprint1.sh
9: #
10: # Output:
11: #   /tmp/swpp-sprint1-bench/
12: #     step-N-<hash>/benchmark_costs.md   per-step results
13: #     step-N-<hash>.done                 completion marker
14: #     build-<hash>.log                   build log per step
15: #     summary.md                         aggregated comparison table
16: 
17: set -euo pipefail
18: 
19: # ── Configuration ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
20: 
21: WORKSPACE="$HOME/workspace"
22: WORK_DIR="/tmp/swpp-sprint1-bench"
23: INTERPRETER_DIR="/tmp/swpp-bench/swpp202601-interpreter"
24: BENCHMARKS_DIR="/tmp/swpp-bench/swpp202601-benchmarks"
25: COST_MODEL="$WORKSPACE/test/tm_cost_static.json"
26: RUN_BENCHMARKS="$WORKSPACE/test/run_benchmarks.py"
27: INTERPRETER_BIN="$INTERPRETER_DIR/target/release/main"
28: TIMEOUT=10
29: 
30: # ── Steps ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
31: # Format: "step_label|commit_hash|description"
32: 
33: STEPS=(
34:   "step-0-7a0cb69|7a0cb69|Baseline (pre-Sprint 1)"
35:   "step-1-8f038c1|8f038c1|#5 Diamond If-Conversion"
36:   "step-2-37d3772|37d3772|#7 Odd-add lowering"
37:   "step-3-3b6703c|3b6703c|#16 mem2reg"
38:   "step-4-d2bfaff|d2bfaff|#11 Loop phi parity"
39:   "step-5-09e10a0|09e10a0|#12 Branch reordering"
40:   "step-6-d9d52d0|d9d52d0|#13 remove_const4 (later reverted)"
41:   "step-7-f4d89f1|f4d89f1|#20 Revert remove_const4"
42:   "step-8-faf007e|faf007e|#15 mul-by-4"
43:   "step-9-7092cb1|7092cb1|#10 Load reordering"
44: )
45: 
46: TOTAL=${#STEPS[@]}
47: 
48: # ── Helpers ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
49: 
50: log() { echo "[$(date '+%H:%M:%S')] $*"; }
51: 
52: check_prereqs() {
53:   if [ ! -f "$INTERPRETER_BIN" ]; then
54:     log "ERROR: interpreter binary not found at $INTERPRETER_BIN"
55:     log "Run the normal benchmark script once first to build it."
56:     exit 1
57:   fi
58:   if [ ! -d "$BENCHMARKS_DIR" ]; then
59:     log "ERROR: benchmarks repo not found at $BENCHMARKS_DIR"
60:     log "Run the normal benchmark script once first to clone them."
61:     exit 1
62:   fi
63:   if [ ! -f "$RUN_BENCHMARKS" ]; then
64:     log "ERROR: run_benchmarks.py not found at $RUN_BENCHMARKS"
65:     exit 1
66:   fi
67: }
68: 
69: build_compiler() {
70:   local commit="$1"
71:   local worktree_dir="$WORK_DIR/src-$commit"
72:   local build_log="$WORK_DIR/build-${commit}.log"
73:   local binary="$worktree_dir/build/swpp-compiler"
74: 
75:   log "  Checking out $commit..."
76:   if [ ! -d "$worktree_dir" ]; then
77:     git -C "$WORKSPACE" worktree add "$worktree_dir" "$commit" \
78:       > "$build_log" 2>&1 \
79:       || { log "  FAIL: worktree add failed. See $build_log"; return 1; }
80:   fi
81: 
82:   log "  Building..."
83:   # compile.sh auto-detects all LLVM flags and builds to $worktree_dir/build/
84:   bash "$worktree_dir/compile.sh" \
85:     >> "$build_log" 2>&1 \
86:     || { log "  FAIL: compile.sh failed. See $build_log"; return 1; }
87: 
88:   if [ ! -f "$binary" ]; then
89:     log "  FAIL: binary not found at $binary after build"
90:     return 1
91:   fi
92: 
93:   log "  Binary: $binary"
94: }
95: 
96: run_benchmarks() {
97:   local compiler="$1"
98:   local out_dir="$2"
99:   local run_log="$out_dir/run.log"
100: 
101:   mkdir -p "$out_dir"
102:   python3 "$RUN_BENCHMARKS" \
103:     --compiler "$compiler" \
104:     --work-dir "$out_dir" \
105:     --cost-model "$COST_MODEL" \
106:     --skip-interpreter-build \
107:     --interpreter-dir "$INTERPRETER_DIR" \
108:     --benchmarks-dir "$BENCHMARKS_DIR" \
109:     --timeout "$TIMEOUT" \
110:     > "$run_log" 2>&1
111:   tail -1 "$run_log"
112: }
113: 
114: # ── Summary generator ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
115: 
116: generate_summary() {
117:   log "Generating summary..."
118: 
119:   local first_csv=""
120:   for entry in "${STEPS[@]}"; do
121:     local label
122:     label=$(echo "$entry" | cut -d'|' -f1)
123:     local csv="$WORK_DIR/$label/benchmark_costs.csv"
124:     if [ -f "$csv" ]; then
125:       first_csv="$csv"
126:       break
127:     fi
128:   done
129: 
130:   if [ -z "$first_csv" ]; then
131:     log "No completed steps found; skipping summary."
132:     return
133:   fi
134: 
135:   python3 - "$WORK_DIR" "${STEPS[@]}" <<'PYEOF'
136: import sys, csv, os
137: 
138: work_dir = sys.argv[1]
139: steps = [s.split("|") for s in sys.argv[2:]]
140: 
141: # Benchmark names from first available CSV
142: first_csv = None
143: for label, commit, desc in steps:
144:     p = os.path.join(work_dir, label, "benchmark_costs.csv")
145:     if os.path.exists(p):
146:         first_csv = p
147:         break
148: 
149: benchmarks = []
150: with open(first_csv) as f:
151:     for row in csv.DictReader(f):
152:         benchmarks.append(row["benchmark"])
153: 
154: # interpreter_cost per step per benchmark
155: data = {}
156: for label, commit, desc in steps:
157:     p = os.path.join(work_dir, label, "benchmark_costs.csv")
158:     if not os.path.exists(p):
159:         data[label] = None
160:         continue
161:     d = {}
162:     with open(p) as f:
163:         for row in csv.DictReader(f):
164:             d[row["benchmark"]] = row.get("interpreter_cost", "")
165:     data[label] = d
166: 
167: out = os.path.join(work_dir, "summary.md")
168: with open(out, "w") as f:
169:     f.write("# Sprint 1 Per-PR Benchmark Summary\n\n")
170:     f.write("Interpreter cost = sum of Final Cost across all passing inputs. ")
171:     f.write("Delta % is relative to the previous step. — = step not completed.\n\n")
172: 
173:     header = "| Benchmark |"
174:     sep    = "| --- |"
175:     for label, commit, desc in steps:
176:         header += f" {desc} `{commit[:7]}` |"
177:         sep    += " --- |"
178:     f.write(header + "\n")
179:     f.write(sep + "\n")
180: 
181:     for bm in benchmarks:
182:         row = f"| {bm} |"
183:         prev_cost = None
184:         for label, commit, desc in steps:
185:             if data[label] is None:
186:                 row += " — |"
187:                 continue
188:             cost_str = data[label].get(bm, "")
189:             if cost_str == "TIMEOUT":
190:                 cell = "TIMEOUT"
191:                 prev_cost = None
192:             elif cost_str == "":
193:                 cell = "—"
194:             else:
195:                 cost = int(cost_str)
196:                 if prev_cost is not None and prev_cost > 0:
197:                     delta = cost - prev_cost
198:                     pct = delta / prev_cost * 100
199:                     sign = "+" if delta >= 0 else ""
200:                     cell = f"{cost:,} ({sign}{pct:.1f}%)"
201:                 else:
202:                     cell = f"{cost:,}"
203:                 prev_cost = cost
204:             row += f" {cell} |"
205:         f.write(row + "\n")
206: 
207: print(f"Summary written to {out}")
208: PYEOF
209: }
210: 
211: # ── Main ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
212: 
213: mkdir -p "$WORK_DIR"
214: check_prereqs
215: 
216: log "Sprint 1 per-PR benchmark — $TOTAL steps"
217: log "Work dir: $WORK_DIR"
218: log ""
219: 
220: idx=0
221: for entry in "${STEPS[@]}"; do
222:   idx=$((idx + 1))
223:   label=$(echo "$entry" | cut -d'|' -f1)
224:   commit=$(echo "$entry" | cut -d'|' -f2)
225:   desc=$(echo "$entry" | cut -d'|' -f3)
226: 
227:   worktree_dir="$WORK_DIR/src-$commit"
228:   binary="$worktree_dir/build/swpp-compiler"
229:   out_dir="$WORK_DIR/$label"
230:   done_marker="$WORK_DIR/$label.done"
231: 
232:   log "[$idx/$TOTAL] $desc ($commit)"
233: 
234:   if [ -f "$done_marker" ]; then
235:     log "  [SKIP] already done"
236:     continue
237:   fi
238: 
239:   log "  [BUILD]"
240:   if ! build_compiler "$commit"; then
241:     log "  [FAIL] build — skipping run"
242:     continue
243:   fi
244:   log "  [BUILD] done"
245: 
246:   log "  [RUN]"
247:   if ! run_benchmarks "$binary" "$out_dir"; then
248:     log "  [FAIL] run — check $out_dir/run.log"
249:     continue
250:   fi
251:   log "  [RUN] done"
252: 
253:   touch "$done_marker"
254:   log "  [DONE]"
255:   log ""
256: done
257: 
258: generate_summary
259: log ""
260: log "Finished."
261: log "  Per-step : $WORK_DIR/step-N-<hash>/benchmark_costs.md"
262: log "  Summary  : $WORK_DIR/summary.md"

# CLAUDE.md

Behavioral guidelines for the SWPP 2026-1 compiler optimization project.

---

## 1. Think Before Coding

**Don't assume. Surface tradeoffs. Ask when uncertain.**

- State assumptions explicitly before implementing.
- If multiple approaches exist, present them — don't pick silently.
- If something is unclear or risky, stop and name it.
- Prefer one correct pass over a fast but broken one. Compiler bugs are silent and hard to debug.

---

## 2. Surgical Changes

**Touch only what you must.**

- Scope: `src/lib/opt.cpp` and files under `src/lib/opt/`. Everything else is out of scope — do not modify it.
  Only those files and under test/ files are ok to be modified.
- Don't "improve" adjacent code, formatting, or unrelated logic.
- Match existing code style exactly.
- Every changed line should trace directly to the task.

---

## 3. This Target Machine Is Not Normal

**The standard LLVM cost model does not apply here. Always reason from the TM spec.**

Key cost facts to internalize:

| Instruction | Cost |
|---|---|
| `br` (true / forward) | 90 (×1.5 if forward jump) |
| `br` (false) | 30 |
| `select` | 3 |
| `mul` | 2 (0 if immediately followed by add/sub) |
| `add` / `sub` (eadd/oadd) | 10 if parity matches, 20 otherwise |
| `icmp` | 3 |
| Any instruction with literal `4` | +10 penalty |

**Do not apply optimizations that are "generally good" unless they're profitable on this machine.** When in doubt, compute the cost delta explicitly before recommending a transformation.

---

## 4. This Is a Team Project

**Coordinate. Never duplicate. Never break teammates' invariants.**

Team member assignments (from `team_sprint_planning.pdf`):

| Member | Sprint 1 | Sprint 2 | Sprint 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 이우진 (me) | Diamond If-Conversion | Triangle If-Conversion | Mul-Add Fusion |
| 윤성원 | Load Spacing | Malloc Splitting | Access Width Opt |
| 유주성 | Parity-aware Lowering | Branch Reordering | Parity/CFG Refinement |
| 양동환 | 4-Phobia Mitigation | Cheap-ALU Rewriting | Heat-Decaying NOP Insertion |

**Before suggesting any optimization, check this table.** If a teammate owns that area, do not touch it — even if the opportunity looks obvious. Suggest coordination instead.

My passes (if-conversion, mul-add fusion) run before parity lowering and branch reordering. Ensure transformations I emit are well-formed IR that downstream passes can process cleanly.

---

## 5. Key Reference Files (under references)

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `references/SWPP202601_TM_Specification.pdf` | Ground truth for instruction costs, calling convention, memory model, 4-phobia, heat, etc. |
| `references/SWPP202601_Project_Specification.pdf` | PR format, grading rubric, doc requirements, CI rules. |

---

## 6. Optimization Discipline

**Correctness over performance. A miscompilation is worse than no optimization.**

For every pass:
1. Write the structural check first — pattern must match exactly before transforming.
2. Write the safety check second — all speculated instructions must be side-effect-free (`isSafeToSpeculativelyExecute()`).
3. Transform only after both checks pass.
4. Cover edge cases explicitly: faulting loads, division by zero, extra phi predecessors, loop back-edges.

For if-conversion specifically:
- Reject any diamond/triangle where a branch block contains: `store`, `call`, `load` (Sprint 1), `div`/`rem` with non-constant-nonzero divisor, or any instruction where `isSafeToSpeculativelyExecute() = false`.
- Verify merge block has **exactly** the expected number of predecessors before replacing phis with selects.

For mul-add fusion:
- The fused add/sub must be the **very next** instruction after mul in emitted order. Run this pass as late as possible.
- Skip any candidate whose operands include the literal constant `4` (4-phobia makes fusion pointless).

---

## 7. Build & Test

# Every code change:
cmake --build build --target swpp-compiler
LLVM_ROOT=~/opt/llvm-22.1.0 ./format-all.sh

# Run tests:
ctest --test-dir build

# Run benchmarks:
bash compile.sh 2>&1 | tail -5
python3 test/run_benchmarks.py --compiler build/swpp-compiler \
   --work-dir ~/swpp-bench --skip-interpreter-build \
  --cost-model test/tm_cost_static.json --timeout 10 2>&1

---

## Pre-PR Checklist

Run these in order inside Docker before every PR:

0. LLVM_ROOT=~/opt/llvm-22.1.0 ./format-all.sh
1. cmake --build build --target swpp-compiler
2. ctest --test-dir build
3. Sanity-check transformed IR by eye:
   /opt/llvm-22.1.0/bin/opt \
     -load-pass-plugin=build/src/lib/opt/SWPPOptPlugin.so \
     -passes=swpp-opt -S <your_test.ll>
4. python3 test/run_benchmarks.py \
     --compiler build/swpp-compiler \
     --work-dir build/benchmarks
5. cat build/benchmarks/benchmark_costs.md
   -> paste results into PR performance table

All 5 steps must pass before opening a PR.

---

## 8. PR Standards

Every PR must have (from project spec):
- **Issue description** — what problem or missed optimization exists.
- **Algorithm** — high-level pseudo-code or clear explanation of the transformation logic.
- **Performance evaluation** — quantitative results on benchmarks + brief analysis.
- **Unit tests** — at least 3, as LLVM IR + FileCheck. Run via `ctest --test-dir build`.
- **Example program tests** — full suite must pass with no regressions.
- **Correctness discussion** — safety conditions, edge cases, intentional limitations.
- Title must start with `[Sprint X]`.
- Must pass CI at time of merge. Squash-merge only (you merge your own PRs).
- 2+ human reviewers required. AI review does not count.

---

## 9. Simplicity First

**Minimum code that solves the problem.**

- No speculative abstractions.
- No handling of cases beyond the current sprint's scope.
- If a helper already exists in LLVM (e.g., `isSafeToSpeculativelyExecute`, `replaceAllUsesWith`), use it — don't reimplement it.
- If an implementation exceeds ~500 lines diff (excl. tests/comments), split it into smaller PRs.

function.name: bash
function.description:
Executes a given bash command in a persistent shell session with optional timeout, ensuring proper handling and security measures.

Be aware: OS: darwin, Shell: zsh

All commands run in the current working directory by default. Use the `workdir` parameter if you need to run a command in a different directory. AVOID using `cd <directory> && <command>` patterns - use `workdir` instead.

Use `/var/folders/55/h73l3pns0t7blyz9c6skr8xm0000gn/T/opencode` for temporary work outside the workspace. This directory has already been created, already exists, and is pre-approved for external directory access.

IMPORTANT: This tool is for terminal operations like git, npm, docker, etc. DO NOT use it for file operations (reading, writing, editing, searching, finding files) - use the specialized tools for this instead.

Before executing the command, please follow these steps:

1. Directory Verification:
   - If the command will create new directories or files, first use `ls` to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location
   - For example, before running "mkdir foo/bar", first use `ls foo` to check that "foo" exists and is the intended parent directory

2. Command Execution:
   - Always quote file paths that contain spaces with double quotes (e.g., rm "path with spaces/file.txt")
   - Examples of proper quoting:
     - mkdir "/Users/name/My Documents" (correct)
     - mkdir /Users/name/My Documents (incorrect - will fail)
     - python "/path/with spaces/script.py" (correct)
     - python /path/with spaces/script.py (incorrect - will fail)
   - After ensuring proper quoting, execute the command.
   - Capture the output of the command.

Usage notes:
  - The command argument is required.
  - You can specify an optional timeout in milliseconds. If not specified, commands will time out after 120000ms.
  - It is very helpful if you write a clear, concise description of what this command does in 5-10 words.
  - If the output exceeds 2000 lines or 51200 bytes, it will be truncated and the full output will be written to a file. You can use Read with offset/limit to read specific sections or Grep to search the full content. Do NOT use `head`, `tail`, or other truncation commands to limit output; the full output will already be captured to a file for more precise searching.

  - Avoid using Bash with the `find`, `grep`, `cat`, `head`, `tail`, `sed`, `awk`, or `echo` commands, unless explicitly instructed or when these commands are truly necessary for the task. Instead, always prefer using the dedicated tools for these commands:
    - File search: Use Glob (NOT find or ls)
    - Content search: Use Grep (NOT grep or rg)
    - Read files: Use Read (NOT cat/head/tail)
    - Edit files: Use Edit (NOT sed/awk)
    - Write files: Use Write (NOT echo >/cat <<EOF)
    - Communication: Output text directly (NOT echo/printf)
  - When issuing multiple commands:
    - If the commands are independent and can run in parallel, make multiple bash tool calls in a single message. For example, if you need to run "git status" and "git diff", send a single message with two bash tool calls in parallel.
    - If the commands depend on each other and must run sequentially, use a single Bash call with '&&' to chain them together (e.g., `git add . && git commit -m "message" && git push`). For instance, if one operation must complete before another starts (like mkdir before cp, Write before Bash for git operations, or git add before git commit), run these operations sequentially instead.
    - Use ';' only when you need to run commands sequentially but don't care if earlier commands fail
    - DO NOT use newlines to separate commands (newlines are ok in quoted strings)
  - AVOID using `cd <directory> && <command>`. Use the `workdir` parameter to change directories instead.
    <good-example>
    Use workdir="/foo/bar" with command: pytest tests
    </good-example>
    <bad-example>
    cd /foo/bar && pytest tests
    </bad-example>

# Git and GitHub
- Only commit, amend, push, or create PRs when explicitly requested.
- Before committing, inspect `git status`, `git diff`, and `git log --oneline -10`; stage only intended files and never commit secrets.
- Write a concise commit message that matches the repo style.
- Do not update git config, skip hooks, use interactive `-i`, force-push, or create empty commits unless explicitly requested.
- If a commit fails or hooks reject it, fix the issue and create a new commit; do not amend the failed commit.
- Before creating a PR, inspect status, diff, remote tracking, recent commits, and the diff from the base branch.
- Review all commits included in the PR, not just the latest commit.
- Use `gh` for GitHub tasks, including PRs, issues, checks, and releases; return the PR URL when done.

workdir.description: The working directory to run the command in. Defaults to the current directory. Use this instead of 'cd' commands.

description.description:
Clear, concise description of what this command does in 5-10 words. Examples:
Input: ls
Output: Lists files in current directory

Input: git status
Output: Shows working tree status

Input: npm install
Output: Installs package dependencies

Input: mkdir foo
Output: Creates directory 'foo'

function.name: glob
function.description:
- Fast file pattern matching tool that works with any codebase size
- Supports glob patterns like "**/*.js" or "src/**/*.ts"
- Returns matching file paths
- Use this tool when you need to find files by name patterns
- When you are doing an open-ended search that may require multiple rounds of globbing and grepping, use the Task tool instead
- You have the capability to call multiple tools in a single response. It is always better to speculatively perform multiple searches as a batch that are potentially useful.

pattern.description: The glob pattern to match files against

path.description: The directory to search in. If not specified, the current working directory will be used. IMPORTANT: Omit this field to use the default directory. DO NOT enter "undefined" or "null" - simply omit it for the default behavior. Must be a valid directory path if provided.

function.name: grep
function.description:
- Fast content search tool that works with any codebase size
- Searches file contents using regular expressions
- Supports full regex syntax (eg. "log.*Error", "function\s+\w+", etc.)
- Filter files by pattern with the include parameter (eg. "*.js", "*.{ts,tsx}")
- Returns file paths and line numbers with matching lines
- Use this tool when you need to find files containing specific patterns
- If you need to identify/count the number of matches within files, use the Bash tool with `rg` (ripgrep) directly. Do NOT use `grep`.
- When you are doing an open-ended search that may require multiple rounds of globbing and grepping, use the Task tool instead

pattern.description: The regex pattern to search for in file contents

path.description: The directory to search in. Defaults to the current working directory.

include.description: File pattern to include in the search (e.g. "*.js", "*.{ts,tsx}")

function.name: read
function.description:
Read a file or directory from the local filesystem. If the path does not exist, an error is returned.

Usage:
- The filePath parameter should be an absolute path.
- By default, this tool returns up to 2000 lines from the start of the file.
- The offset parameter is the line number to start from (1-indexed).
- To read later sections, call this tool again with a larger offset.
- Use the grep tool to find specific content in large files or files with long lines.
- If you are unsure of the correct file path, use the glob tool to look up filenames by glob pattern.
- Contents are returned with each line prefixed by its line number as `<line>: <content>`. For example, if a file has contents "foo\n", you will receive "1: foo\n". For directories, entries are returned one per line (without line numbers) with a trailing `/` for subdirectories.
- Any line longer than 2000 characters is truncated.
- Call this tool in parallel when you know there are multiple files you want to read.
- Avoid tiny repeated slices (30 line chunks). If you need more context, read a larger window.
- This tool can read image files and PDFs and return them as file attachments.

filePath.description: The absolute path to the file or directory to read

offset.description: The line number to start reading from (1-indexed)

limit.description: The maximum number of lines to read (defaults to 2000)

function.name: webfetch
function.description:
- Fetches content from a specified URL
- Takes a URL and optional format as input
- Fetches the URL content, converts to requested format (markdown by default)
- Returns the content in the specified format
- Use this tool when you need to retrieve and analyze web content

Usage notes:
  - IMPORTANT: if another tool is present that offers better web fetching capabilities, is more targeted to the task, or has fewer restrictions, prefer using that tool instead of this one.
  - The URL must be a fully-formed valid URL
  - HTTP URLs will be automatically upgraded to HTTPS
  - Format options: "markdown" (default), "text", or "html"
  - This tool is read-only and does not modify any files
  - Results may be summarized if the content is very large

url.description: The URL to fetch content from

format.description: The format to return the content in (text, markdown, or html). Defaults to markdown.
